Q1: I tried to set up a consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after I'd dealt with everything else during the day. I'd block out time to read through materials and take notes, mostly handwritten because that's how things stick for me. Honestly though, my attendance was really poor this semester, so a lot of my studying happened independently rather than in class. I'd spend maybe two to three hours at a stretch when I could focus, aiming for around 17 hours a week total.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I wanted it to. I'd start each week with good intentions to study at set times, but then deadlines would pile up and I'd end up cramming. Some weeks I was really disciplined, other weeks life got chaotic and I'd have to adjust everything. I think I responded more to pressure than to any internal schedule I'd created.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or download materials I hadn't grabbed yet. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a new resource being uploaded, and that would prompt me to check in. Honestly, it wasn't a daily habit for me—more like when I remembered or when something specific required my attention.
Q4: There were a few weeks early in the semester where checking the LMS became somewhat automatic, usually in the morning with my coffee. But that routine kind of fell apart when I started missing classes and felt disconnected from the course rhythm. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me get back into some routines, but the LMS habit never fully solidified for me.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me since I learn best by reading and writing things out. I also found the video lectures helpful—I'd watch them at my own pace and pause to take notes. The discussion forums were okay when people actually engaged meaningfully, because seeing other perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with.
Q6: I often skipped the optional supplementary readings and some of the interactive activities that felt more like busywork than actual learning. If something didn't seem directly connected to assignments or exams, I'd push it aside. I know that's probably not the best approach, but with limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would actually impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to understanding the material better or improving my assignment scores. If I finished something and felt like I actually learned something new or clarified a confusing concept, that was time well spent. Activities that felt repetitive or too basic for where I was at just frustrated me.
Q8: Required activities always had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them regardless of how I felt. Optional ones were easier to skip when I was stressed or tired, even if part of me knew they might be helpful. There's something about the pressure of requirements that actually helped me push through on hard days, whereas optional stuff was the first thing I'd cut when overwhelmed.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then work through them with a notebook beside me, annotating and summarizing in my own words. I also used some external resources—YouTube explanations, other textbooks I found online—especially when the course materials weren't clicking for me. That offline work was probably where most of my actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with printed materials or handwritten notes, really working through problems and concepts, but none of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd spend hours reviewing my own notes before exams, or I'd be discussing concepts with a friend over coffee. The LMS only saw when I clicked on something, not when I was genuinely wrestling with ideas.
Q11: Yeah, definitely. I'd access a lecture video or a PDF once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS version. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform. It was more convenient and let me study on my own terms, but it meant my engagement looked minimal online.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was feeling sad and overwhelmed, and my attendance had dropped so much that I felt disconnected from the course and my classmates. The stress built up and I started doubting whether I could catch up. That's actually when I started the mindfulness practices, which helped me manage the anxiety, but the academic hole I'd dug was deep.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at it without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities—family stuff, trying to work a bit on the side—meant the course sometimes got pushed down my priority list. Confusion with certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely for a while, which wasn't smart but felt necessary for my mental health.
Q14: There were times I'd open a video or reading and technically be 'watching' or 'reading' but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be thinking about everything else going wrong or just feeling too drained to absorb anything. Those sessions looked like engagement in the data, but I'd finish and realize I'd retained almost nothing.
Q15: I postponed studying for the exam way longer than I should have, even knowing how important it was. Part of it was avoidance—the material felt overwhelming and I didn't know where to start after missing so many classes. I'd tell myself I'd start tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. The anxiety made it worse, creating this cycle of avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main driver of my LMS activity. I'd suddenly become very active in the days before something was due, downloading everything, watching videos I'd ignored, posting in discussions. It wasn't ideal, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my procrastination and sadness to get me moving.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it did motivate me to engage more—especially positive feedback that showed I was understanding things correctly. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum. Good feedback made me want to do better on the next thing; poor or missing feedback made me feel like my effort didn't matter much.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace when life got complicated, bad because without strict attendance requirements or regular check-ins, I let myself slip. A more structured approach with mandatory participation might have kept me more accountable, even if I would have resisted it initially.
Q19: The fact that materials were available for download made engagement easier for my learning style. But the discussion forums felt a bit clunky and didn't encourage deep conversation—people would just post to meet requirements. Also, having so many resources available was overwhelming sometimes; I didn't always know what was essential versus supplementary.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand the material but struggling to actually produce work that meets the standard. They might be reading everything, watching all the videos, genuinely trying—but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. High activity doesn't always mean high output, especially when someone's dealing with stress or confusion.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My assignment scores were decent even though my LMS activity wasn't super high, because I did a lot of work offline. If someone is efficient with their time or learns well through methods the LMS can't track, their low activity might not reflect their actual engagement. The system just can't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. That's useful for identifying students who might be struggling or disengaging. But it misses the quality of engagement, the offline work, the emotional state behind the clicks. Someone could be logged in for hours and learning nothing, or logged in for minutes and deeply focused.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's when I'm curious about a topic, when I'm thinking about it even outside of study time, when I want to discuss it with others. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent clicking through a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite feeling sad and overwhelmed. It wouldn't show the mindfulness sessions that helped me manage my anxiety, or the hours I spent with handwritten notes trying to catch up. It can't capture resilience—the fact that even when I was struggling, I kept trying to climb back. My grade says fail, but my effort tells a different story.
